summary: This PR adds range-request support to `send_file` by calling `rv.make_conditional` with `accept_ranges=True` and `complete_length` when werkzeug ≥ 0.12 is detected. The change introduces several correctness issues: `file.close()` can be called on a `None` or already-closed handle in the 416 error path, and `fsize` is never set when a file-object is passed directly, causing `make_conditional` to receive `complete_length=None`. Test coverage for several new and restructured code paths is also lacking.

findings.title: Unsafe file.close() in 416 handler — double-close or AttributeError on None
findings.detail:
The `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` handler unconditionally calls `file.close()`, but there are two dangerous cases:

1. **x-sendfile + filename string path**: when `use_x_sendfile` is True and `filename` is a string, `file` is never assigned a file object (it stays `None` from line 513). Calling `file.close()` will raise `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'close'`, masking the original 416 exception entirely.

2. **x-sendfile + file-object path**: when `use_x_sendfile` is True and the caller passes a file object, the file is already closed earlier (line 541–542). Calling `file.close()` again at line 594 is a double-close on a stale reference.

The fix is to guard with `if file is not None: file.close()` before re-raising, and to ensure the x-sendfile path correctly nulls out `file` after closing it.

findings.title: fsize is None when a file-object is passed, breaking range-request Content-Range headers
findings.detail: The variable `fsize` is initialized to `None` (line 508) and is only assigned when: (a) `use_x_sendfile` is True and a filename exists, or (b) a plain filename string was provided (not a file object). When the caller passes a file-object directly (`filename_or_fp` is a file, so `file = filename_or_fp` at line 517), `fsize` is never populated and remains `None`. The subsequent call `rv.make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=None)` may silently produce incorrect or empty `Content-Range` headers, or raise a `TypeError`/`ValueError` inside werkzeug. No existing test exercises this path (all range-request tests pass a filename string), so it is undetected. The fix is to compute `fsize` via `os.fstat(file.fileno()).st_size` (or equivalent) when a file object is provided.

findings.title: Old-werkzeug fallback branch (else: rv.make_conditional(request)) has no test coverage
findings.detail: The new `if callable(getattr(Range, 'to_content_range_header', None)):` guard splits the conditional path into a ≥0.12 branch and an older-werkzeug fallback. The test for range requests is decorated with `@pytest.mark.skipif(not callable(...))`, meaning it is skipped exactly when the old-werkzeug branch would execute. There is no test that exercises `send_file(conditional=True)` on a werkzeug build where `Range.to_content_range_header` is absent, so the fallback `else` branch at line 597 is completely untested.

findings.title: File-close-on-416 path is not covered by any test
findings.detail: Lines 593-595 close the file and re-raise `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`. The existing 416 test cases verify the HTTP status code on the response but do not assert that the underlying file object is actually closed. There is also no test that triggers a 416 by passing a real file-object as `filename_or_fp` (the path where `file` is set and `.close()` is meaningful). A test using a spy/mock on `file.close()` or a custom file-object subclass should be added to verify this path.

findings.title: x-sendfile + range request interaction is untested
findings.detail: `fsize` is now captured when the x-sendfile path is active and passed as `complete_length` to `make_conditional`. There is no test that issues a range request through `send_file` when `app.use_x_sendfile = True`, so the interaction between the X-Sendfile header and the new range-aware `make_conditional` call is entirely unverified.

findings.title: No test verifies that range headers are ignored when conditional=False
findings.detail: The restructured code moved the `if conditional:` block, changing its scope relative to the `if filename:` guard. There is no test asserting that sending a `Range` request header with `conditional=False` still returns a full 200 response (i.e., range headers are ignored), leaving a potential regression undetected.

findings.title: 304 + x-sendfile header removal not tested after block restructuring
findings.detail: The block that removes the x-sendfile header on a 304 response was moved outside the `if filename:` guard as part of this PR. No test verifies that a 304 response (triggered by a matching ETag or Last-Modified) still strips the x-sendfile header under the new code layout, leaving a potential regression uncovered.

findings.title: Version-guard comment is redundant and inconsistent with surrounding style
findings.detail:
The comment `# Werkzeug supports Range Requests` immediately above the `if callable(...)` check is redundant — the condition itself expresses this. The second line (`# Remove this test when support for Werkzeug <0.12 is dropped`) uses a different capitalisation and phrasing style from other comments nearby. Consider collapsing to a single concise comment, e.g.:
```python
# Range request support added in Werkzeug 0.12; remove this check when
# support for older versions is dropped.
```

findings.title: File handle leaked if make_conditional raises an unexpected exception
findings.detail: The `try/except` only catches `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`. If `rv.make_conditional()` raises any other exception (e.g. an unexpected `ValueError` or werkzeug internal error), `file.close()` is never called, leaking the open file descriptor. Under sustained error conditions this can contribute to resource exhaustion. A `try/finally` block (closing the file in the `finally` clause and only re-raising on error) or a context-manager approach would be safer.

findings.title: Range-request make_conditional called even on x-sendfile path, may produce misleading 206 response
findings.detail: When `use_x_sendfile` is True, the response body (`data`) is set to `None` and the file is closed, but `fsize` is still set and `rv.make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=fsize)` is still called. If werkzeug honours the `Range` header and returns a 206 response with an empty body (since `data=None`), the X-Sendfile server will serve the full file while the HTTP status code says 206 (partial content), producing a misleading and incorrect response. The x-sendfile path should either skip the range-aware `make_conditional` call or explicitly pass `accept_ranges=False`.

findings.title: Typo in skip reason string: "not implement" should be "not implemented"
findings.detail: The `reason` string reads `"not implement within werkzeug"` — it should be `"not implemented in werkzeug"` (or `"not supported by werkzeug"`) to be grammatically correct.

findings.title: Hard-to-parse chained method call; consider splitting into two statements
findings.detail:
The closing `)).replace(` mid-line and trailing `microsecond=0)` make the expression difficult to read. Splitting into two statements improves clarity:
```python
mtime_ts = os.path.getmtime(os.path.join(app.root_path, 'static/index.html'))
last_modified = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(mtime_ts).replace(microsecond=0)
```

findings.title: Abbreviation `fsize` is inconsistent with the variable naming style in this function
findings.detail: `fsize` is an abbreviation that does not appear elsewhere in the codebase. The surrounding variables use full English words (`mtime`, `filename`, `attachment_filename`, etc.). A name like `file_size` or `content_length` would be more readable and consistent.

findings.title: Backslash line continuation in import could use parentheses instead
findings.detail: The new import `from werkzeug.exceptions import BadRequest, NotFound, \ RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` uses backslash continuation. The more Pythonic style is to wrap with parentheses. This is a minor style nit and is arguably consistent with other existing backslash-continued imports in the file.

findings.title: file.close() called on stale reference when x-sendfile path closes the file
findings.detail:
When `use_x_sendfile` is True and a `filename` is given, the code at line 541-542 calls `file.close()` on any file object passed in, then sets `file = None` (via `data = None`). However, `file` is not reassigned to `None` — it still holds the original file object that was already closed. Later, in the `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` handler (line 594), calling `file.close()` on that already-closed handle is a double-close.

Moreover when `filename_or_fp` is a string and `use_x_sendfile` is True, `file` remains `None` (set at line 513), and calling `file.close()` at line 594 will raise `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'close'`, masking the original 416 exception.

The correct approach is to guard with `if file is not None: file.close()` before re-raising, and make sure the x-sendfile path nulls out `file` correctly.

findings.title: fsize is None when file object (not filename) is passed, causing TypeError in make_conditional
findings.detail: `fsize` is only set when: (a) `use_x_sendfile` is True and a filename exists, or (b) `file is None` in the else branch (i.e. a filename was given, not a file object). When the caller passes a file object directly (`file = filename_or_fp`, line 517), `fsize` is never set and stays `None` (line 508). The code then calls `rv.make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=fsize)` with `complete_length=None`. Depending on how werkzeug implements `make_conditional`, this may silently produce wrong `Content-Range` headers or raise a TypeError/ValueError, breaking range request support for file-object inputs.

findings.title: File opened internally is not closed when a 416 range error occurs under x-sendfile path
findings.detail: When `use_x_sendfile` is False and `filename` was provided (string path), the code opens the file internally at line 549 (`file = open(filename, 'rb')`). If `make_conditional` raises `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`, line 594 calls `file.close()` — which is correct for this path. However this is only correct by accident because `file` happens to be the internally-opened handle. If instead `use_x_sendfile` is True and `filename` is a string, `file` is `None` and `file.close()` will raise `AttributeError`, hiding the 416 exception from the caller. These two code paths need separate handling.

findings.title: File handle leaked when make_conditional raises an exception other than RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable
findings.detail: The try/except only catches `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable`. If `rv.make_conditional()` raises any other exception (e.g. a generic ValueError or an unexpected werkzeug exception), `file.close()` is never called, leaking the open file descriptor. A `try/finally` or a broader catch-and-close pattern would be safer. This is also present for the non-range-request path: if `rv.make_conditional(request)` at line 597 raises, the file is also not closed. This doesn't directly create a security vulnerability in typical usage but can contribute to resource exhaustion (DoS) under sustained error conditions.

findings.title: Range request processing occurs even when file was served via X-Sendfile (fsize set but data=None)
findings.detail: When `use_x_sendfile` is True and the X-Sendfile header path is taken (lines 540-546), `data` is set to `None` and the file is closed, but `fsize` is still set. The call to `rv.make_conditional(request, accept_ranges=True, complete_length=fsize)` will proceed with a `None` data body. If werkzeug's `make_conditional` tries to seek or read the response data to satisfy a range request in this code path, it could produce an incorrect or confusing partial-content (206) response with an empty body rather than the expected X-Sendfile behavior. The X-Sendfile server would then serve the full file regardless, so an attacker requesting `Range: bytes=0-0` might receive the full file content from the backend. This is a logic issue that can also result in a misleading 206 response with no body.

findings.title: Backslash line continuation instead of parentheses for import
findings.detail: The existing import style in the file uses parentheses for multi-line imports (e.g., lines 41-42: `from .globals import session, _request_ctx_stack, _app_ctx_stack, \ current_app, request`). However the existing file actually uses backslash there too, so this is consistent. But the import split as `from werkzeug.exceptions import BadRequest, NotFound, \ RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` could instead be written with parentheses for slightly cleaner wrapping — though both forms appear in the codebase. Minor nit only.

findings.title: Version-guard comment could be clearer and more consistent with surrounding comment style
findings.detail:
The comment block at lines 587-589 reads:
```
# Werkzeug supports Range Requests
# Remove this test when support for Werkzeug <0.12 is dropped
```
The surrounding code comments (e.g. line 598-599) use lowercase and more concise phrasing. Additionally, the first comment line (`# Werkzeug supports Range Requests`) is redundant given the `if callable(...)` check immediately above it — the condition already implies that. Consider collapsing to a single comment, e.g.:
```python
# Range request support added in Werkzeug 0.12; remove this check when
# support for older versions is dropped.
```

findings.title: `fsize = None` initialisation placement is slightly inconsistent
findings.detail: `mtime = None` and `fsize = None` are initialised on consecutive lines, which is fine. However `mtime` is only set within one specific branch and `fsize` likewise. Grouping them together is reasonable but `fsize` is named differently from the surrounding convention where file-size is referred to via `os.path.getsize(filename)` inline. A name like `file_size` or `content_length` would read more clearly and match the natural-English style of the rest of the variable names in the function (`mtime`, `filename`, `attachment_filename`, etc.). `fsize` is an abbreviation that doesn't appear anywhere else in the codebase.

findings.title: Typo in skip reason string: "not implement" should be "not implemented"
findings.detail: The `reason` string reads `"not implement within werkzeug"` — it should be `"not implemented in werkzeug"` (or `"not supported by werkzeug"`) to be grammatically correct and consistent with the professional tone of the rest of the test file.

findings.title: Long chained method call formatting is inconsistent with surrounding test style
findings.detail:
Lines 516-518:
```python
last_modified = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(os.path.getmtime(
    os.path.join(app.root_path, 'static/index.html'))).replace(
    microsecond=0)
```
The closing `)).replace(` and `microsecond=0)` at the end of the chain is hard to parse. Other multi-line expressions in the test file (e.g. the `c.get` calls on lines 520-521 and 525-526) keep the continuation argument indented consistently under the opening parenthesis. Consider splitting this into two statements for clarity:
```python
mtime_ts = os.path.getmtime(os.path.join(app.root_path, 'static/index.html'))
last_modified = datetime.datetime.fromtimestamp(mtime_ts).replace(microsecond=0)
```

findings.title: Old-werkzeug fallback path (`else: rv.make_conditional(request)`) has no test coverage
findings.detail: The new conditional block at line 586 splits into two branches: one for werkzeug ≥0.12 (range-request path) and one for older werkzeug (the plain `rv.make_conditional(request)` fallback). The test is entirely guarded by `@pytest.mark.skipif(not callable(getattr(Range, 'to_content_range_header', None)), ...)`, meaning it is skipped precisely when the old-werkzeug branch would execute. There is no test that exercises `send_file(conditional=True)` on a werkzeug version where `Range.to_content_range_header` is absent, so the fallback `else` branch at line 597 is never covered in any tested scenario.

findings.title: File-close-on-416 path is not covered by any test
findings.detail: Lines 593-595 handle `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` by closing the file and re-raising. The existing 416 test cases (e.g., `bytes=1000-`, `bytes=-`, `somethingsomething`) verify the HTTP status code on the *response*, but the file object used internally is opened inside the route closure each time. There is no test that verifies the file is actually closed when a 416 is raised (e.g., using a spy/mock on `file.close()`), and no test sends a range request through `send_file` when a real file object is passed as `filename_or_fp` (which is when `file` is set and `.close()` on it is meaningful).

findings.title: No test for range request when `conditional=False` (unchanged behaviour)
findings.detail: The restructured code moved the `if conditional:` block outside the `if filename:` guard, changing its scope. There is no test asserting that sending a range request header while `conditional=False` still returns a full 200 response (i.e., range headers are ignored), which would protect against regressions in the restructured scope.

findings.title: x-sendfile + range request interaction is not tested
findings.detail: `fsize` is now captured when x-sendfile is active (line 544) and passed as `complete_length` to `make_conditional`. There is no test that issues a range request through `send_file` when `app.use_x_sendfile = True`, so the interaction between the x-sendfile header and the new range-aware `make_conditional` call is unverified.

findings.title: 304 + x-sendfile header removal not tested after the block restructuring
findings.detail: The block that removes the x-sendfile header on a 304 response was moved outside the `if filename:` guard as part of this PR. There is no test that verifies a 304 response (triggered by a matching ETag/Last-Modified) still strips the x-sendfile header under the new code layout.

findings.title: No test for range request on a file-object (fp) rather than a filename string
findings.detail: All range-request tests in `test_send_file_range_request` call `send_file('static/index.html', ...)` with a filename string. The code path where `filename_or_fp` is a file-object (`fsize` remains `None`, so `complete_length=None` is passed to `make_conditional`) is not exercised by the new tests.

findings.detail:
The `RequestedRangeNotSatisfiable` handler unconditionally calls `file.close()`, but there are two dangerous cases:

1. **x-sendfile + filename string path**: when `use_x_sendfile` is True and `filename` is a string, `file` is never assigned a file object (it stays `None` from line 513). There is no test that issues a range request through `send_file` when `app.use_x_sendfile = True`, so the interaction between the X-Sendfile header and the new range-aware `make_conditional` call is entirely unverified.
